Web development: Excels in full‑stack web development, producing clean, production‑ready code with minimal guidance. Legal reasoning: supports interpretation of contracts, case law, and regulatory documents. When deployed through Foundry, Azure AI Content Safety is enabled by default, adding an additional layer of protection for enterprise use. Customers can review the Microsoft Foundry model card for detailed safety and usage considerations. Microsoft Foundry also provides tools to support our customers with their responsible AI efforts, including model cards during selection, configurable guardrails such as jailbreak detection and content filtering, pre‑deployment evaluations and red teaming, and post‑deployment monitoring and governance. These capabilities help customers maintain output quality and deploy Grok 4.3 responsibly at scale. record.text: NVIDIA Dynamo on AKS - Autoscaling LLM Inference

external.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly moving from experimentation to production. While training often gets the spotlight, inference at scale is where real-world complexity emerges—especially when performance, cost, and user experience must all be balanced under unpredictable traffic. In this post, we’ll walk through how Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) combined with NVIDIA Dynamo enables scalable, production‑ready LLM inference, and how to think about autoscaling GPUs effectively for modern AI workloads. Why LLM Inference Autoscaling Is Hard 1. Variable and Non-Linear Metrics Each request can vary significantly in compute cost depending on: Model size and precision Input and output token length KV cache hit rate GPU or memory utilization alone is not enough to represent real load. 2. Multi-Phase Inference Pipelines LLM inference is not a single step. It consists of: Prefill (context processing) Routing Decode (token generation) Each phase has distinct performance characteristics and resource requirements, making coordinated scaling essential. 3. GPU Autoscaling Constraints GPUs are: Slower to provision than CPUs Less elastic Less available & more expensive Efficient packing, warm capacity, and informed scaling decisions are critical. Choosing the Right Azure GPU Infrastructure Azure offers a broad AI‑optimized VM portfolio designed for inference and training workloads. Selecting the right GPU SKU depends on: Model size Throughput requirements Latency SLOs Cost targets AKS allows these GPUs to be managed through autoscaling node pools, forming the foundation for elastic inference platforms. NVIDIA Dynamo on AKS: The Core Building Blocks NVIDIA Dynamo is designed specifically for production LLM workload. It has several key capabilities: Smart Routing – Routes requests efficiently across workers KV Cache Management – Improves reuse and memory efficiency Low-Latency Communication – Optimized GPU‑to‑GPU and service communication GPU Planner – Coordinates capacity decisions across the pipeline Together, these components enable Dynamo to treat inference as a system, not just a set of pods. The “Autoscaling Magic Sauce”: Dynamo + AKS What makes this architecture powerful is how Dynamo integrates natively with Kubernetes and Azure: - Dynamo Scaling Adapters Dynamo exposes Kubernetes scale subresources, allowing it to work seamlessly with: Kubernetes HPA KEDA Dynamo Planner Custom controllers - AKS GPU Node Pool Autoscaling AKS automatically scales GPU node pools based on pending pod demand using the cluster autoscaler. - Observability Built In By integrating with Azure Managed Prometheus and Grafana, you get: Real-time inference metrics Visibility into latency, throughput, and queue depth Data-driven scaling decisions Four Ways to Autoscale Dynamo Workloads Dynamo supports multiple autoscaling strategies: 1. Kubernetes HPA Best suited for: Simple CPU or memory‑based scaling Frontend or lightweight services 2. KEDA (Event-Driven Autoscaling) Ideal when: External or custom metrics are required Scale‑to‑zero Preset fixed max number of replicas 3. Dynamo Planner (LLM‑Aware Autoscaling) Designed specifically for production LLM inference: Adaptive scaling - no preset max number of replicas Disaggregated scaling of prefill and decode workers Uses live metrics and profiling data Supports SLA‑driven goals (e.g., TTFT < 500ms) 4. Custom Controllers Used for advanced or bespoke scaling logic when standard options are insufficient. Metric Alignment: Scaling the Right Way A key lesson in production inference is metric alignment—each service type should scale on signals that actually reflect pressure: Frontend Services CPU utilization Request rate Prefill Workers Queue depth Time to First Token (TTFT) Decode Workers KV cache utilization Inter-token latency (ITL) When metrics match service behavior, scaling becomes both faster and more stable. Avoiding Common Autoscaling Pitfalls A few best practices can save weeks of troubleshooting: Use more than one autoscaler per service Avoid multiple autoscalers fighting over the same workload. mis-configure stabilization windows Prevent rapid scale‑down that causes thrashing. Arbitrary min/max replicas Protect the cluster from unbounded scaling or accidental scale‑to‑zero. Understand your traffic patterns Short, spiky workloads require different strategies than steady-state serving. Example: TTFT‑Driven Autoscaling with KEDA (Aggregated Serving) This example shows NVIDIA Dynamo aggregated serving on AKS, where Prefill and Decode run in the same GPU worker pod. Autoscaling is handled by KEDA, using TTFT p95 latency from Prometheus as the scaling signal instead of CPU or GPU utilization. Configuration summary Serving mode: Aggregated (Prefill + Decode per pod) Autoscaler: KEDA Scaling signal: TTFT p95 > 300 ms Worker replicas: 2 → 4 Scale‑down cooldown: 120 seconds GPU: NC‑H100 What this load represents Model: Qwen3‑0.6B, small but latency‑sensitive under long prompts Traffic pattern: Constant request rate (36 RPS) with no traffic drop‑off Context pressure: Large input size (≈3000 tokens) to stress Prefill Streaming enabled: TTFT becomes the dominant user‑perceived metric Duration: Long‑running test to expose cold vs warm scale‑up behavior What the chart shows The chart below plots TTFT p95 over time while traffic remains constant. Event Timeline TimeEventTTFT P95 Behavior~14:35First scale‑up triggeredThe constant load saturates the 2 baseline workers. KEDA detects TTFT p95 > 300 ms and requests 2 additional VllmDecodeWorker pods.14:35 → 14:43Cold node provisioning (~8 min)New GPU nodes must be provisioned by Azure. The GPU Operator installs drivers and Dynamo loads the model. No new decode capacity is available yet, so TTFT continues to rise.~14:43New pods start runningThe 2 additional workers come online. With 4 GPUs serving the same load, TTFT p95 drops sharply.~14:53First scale‑down attemptTTFT p95 falls below 300 ms, so KEDA scales down back to 2 workers after the 120 s cooldown. Traffic is unchanged, so the remaining workers are immediately overloaded and TTFT spikes again.~14:57Second scale‑upKEDA re‑triggers scale‑up. This completes quickly because AKS has not yet de‑provisioned the GPU nodes — they are still warm (drivers loaded, model cached). TTFT drops rapidly.~15:04Second scale‑down attemptThe same pattern repeats: capacity is reduced, TTFT rises under constant traffic.~15:07Third scale‑upScale‑up is fast again for the same reason — nodes are still warm. The TTFT spike is shorter than during the initial cold start. Warm vs. cold scale‑ups The second and third scale‑ups are fast only because AKS has not yet terminated the extra GPU nodes. If the nodes had been removed, the next scale‑up would again incur the ~10‑minute cold‑start penalty. Notebook & Code: Dynamo_on_AKS/autoscaling_agg_keda/dynamo-keda.ipynb at main · maljazaery/Dynamo_on_AKS How can I implement this? Step by step notebooks available here: maljazaery/Dynamo_on_AKS: End-to-end tutorials for running NVIDIA Dynamo on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)

Example: TTFT‑Driven Autoscaling with KEDA (Aggregated Serving) This example shows NVIDIA Dynamo aggregated serving on AKS, where Prefill and Decode run in the same GPU worker pod. Autoscaling is handled by KEDA, using TTFT p95 latency from Prometheus as the scaling signal instead of CPU or GPU utilization. Configuration summary Serving mode: Aggregated (Prefill + Decode per pod) Autoscaler: KEDA Scaling signal: TTFT p95 > 300 ms Worker replicas: 2 → 4 Scale‑down cooldown: 120 seconds GPU: NC‑H100 What this load represents Model: Qwen3‑0.6B, small but latency‑sensitive under long prompts Traffic pattern: Constant request rate (36 RPS) with no traffic drop‑off Context pressure: Large input size (≈3000 tokens) to stress Prefill Streaming enabled: TTFT becomes the dominant user‑perceived metric Duration: Long‑running test to expose cold vs warm scale‑up behavior What the chart shows The chart below plots TTFT p95 over time while traffic remains constant. Event Timeline TimeEventTTFT P95 Behavior~14:35First scale‑up triggeredThe constant load saturates the 2 baseline workers. KEDA detects TTFT p95 > 300 ms and requests 2 additional VllmDecodeWorker pods.14:35 → 14:43Cold node provisioning (~8 min)New GPU nodes must be provisioned by Azure. The GPU Operator installs drivers and Dynamo loads the model. No new decode capacity is available yet, so TTFT continues to rise.~14:43New pods start runningThe 2 additional workers come online. With 4 GPUs serving the same load, TTFT p95 drops sharply.~14:53First scale‑down attemptTTFT p95 falls below 300 ms, so KEDA scales down back to 2 workers after the 120 s cooldown. Traffic is unchanged, so the remaining workers are immediately overloaded and TTFT spikes again.~14:57Second scale‑upKEDA re‑triggers scale‑up. This completes quickly because AKS has not yet de‑provisioned the GPU nodes — they are still warm (drivers loaded, model cached). TTFT drops rapidly.~15:04Second scale‑down attemptThe same pattern repeats: capacity is reduced, TTFT rises under constant traffic.~15:07Third scale‑upScale‑up is fast again for the same reason — nodes are still warm. The TTFT spike is shorter than during the initial cold start. Warm vs. cold scale‑ups The second and third scale‑ups are fast only because AKS has not yet terminated the extra GPU nodes. If the nodes had been removed, the next scale‑up would again incur the ~10‑minute cold‑start penalty. Notebook & Code: Dynamo_on_AKS/autoscaling_agg_keda/dynamo-keda.ipynb at main · maljazaery/Dynamo_on_AKS How can I implement this? Step by step notebooks available here: maljazaery/Dynamo_on_AKS: End-to-end tutorials for running NVIDIA Dynamo on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
external.title: NVIDIA Dynamo on AKS - Autoscaling LLM Inference

You're playing as a six-year-old dimension-hopper named Allison, and each of the seven episodes throws…

external.description:
Eileen sits down to play Allison of Astra, a homebrew NES game currently crowdfunding on Kickstarter, and it is not what you'd expect. You're playing as a six-year-old dimension-hopper named Allison, and each of the seven episodes throws you into a completely different world with completely different gameplay. One minute it's a robot uprising. The next, you're platforming alongside a living shoe named Booty.

The whole thing runs on original Nintendo hardware, built with NESMaker and the rare Mapper 28 circuit board, packed into 256kb episodes without cutting a single thing. Six years of development from PopGeeks contributor Peter Paltridge.

We play through most of Episode 1, Allison's Doodle Quest, here, and there's a free browser demo linked below if you want to try it yourself before the Kickstarter closes. Backing options start at $30 for a digital ROM all the way up to a red cartridge collector's edition with a certificate of authenticity.

If you've ever loved retro gaming, wondered how homebrew actually gets made, or just want to watch something genuinely surprising, this one's for you. 

Make sure to follow the Kickstarter for behind the scenes updates on Peter's process in making the indie game.

🕹️ Back the campaign on Kickstarter: https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/1637173865/allison-of-astra-an-nes-homebrew-multicart?ref=8u3lc7
🕹️ Get the Demo: https://theretroverse.com/product/allison-of-astra-the-complete-first-season-demo/

record.text:
Acho que o que facilita o uso dela em código em comparação com tradução é que tradução a qualidade do conteúdo gerado é de interesse do público (costumer etc).

Pra computação, o costumer só liga pro resultado final, e o conteúdo gerado é relevante só pro operador.

Na verdade isso é um erro que gemini, gpt, meta AI e copilot cometem o tempo todo.

Claude não comete muito (por isso todo mundo usa ele pra computação), ou ao menos nunca vi...

Dizer que eu já vi caso da mesma coisa rolar com código: A IA criar mais de um dado de input pra mesma coisa, e usar os dois no código regularmente. Não afetou o resultado final, mas ficou um código bem mais poluído.

Microsoft move GitHub Copilot to consumption billing from June 1st, continuing the rise of AI Credits as a new billing mechanism.

#microsoft #licensing #finops

external.description: A group of software developers collaborate around computers with AI interfaces in a modern office From June 1st, GitHub Copilot usage will start to consume GitHub AI Credits. Microsoft say that, as Copilot use has changed and become more complex, inference costs have increased and they can no longer sustain the current premium request unit (PRU) model. Copilot features that consume AI credits include Copilot Chat, Copilot CLI, Copilot cloud agent, Copilot Spaces, Spark, and third-party coding agents.

Yes I can watch on my computer browser with an ad blocker but even there Big G will passive aggressively throttle your connection or downgrade the quality because you're blocking the ads.

I get that this platform costs money. I just think they've screwed up the ratio of ads to content. Because greed.

Yes I know about Loops, Tilvids, third party apps, torrents, etc.

It's called excitation transfer theory and learning about it can make you 🫪

Denkt dann so 8 Minuten nach. Gibt Optionen. Ich gebe Feedback. Er schreibt Spec und UI Mockup als ASCII-Art. Paar Stunden auch, nebenbei.
